nuggets.headline: Aaron Doh's signature atmospheric sound isn't an accident; it's a direct application of the studio philosophy perfected by CHVRCHES' Martin Doherty.
nuggets.text: Doh's entire production methodology on "Love Lies" mirrors the techniques used on CHVRCHES records, emphasizing the extensive layering of synthesizers and meticulous, near-obsessive editing. This approach, detailed in interviews with the band, involves creating a polished, atmospheric sound that balances crisp digital precision with warm, textured layers to achieve a signature sonic fingerprint [CIT 1]. It’s a deliberate choice to build a world of sound rather than just a collection of instruments.
nuggets.imageCaption: Synth-pop trio CHVRCHES, whose production techniques heavily influenced Aaron Doh's sound.

nuggets.headline: The track's deceptively simple groove is built on a modern re-engineering of a classic Motown mixing trick used on the 1976 original.
nuggets.text: Listen closely to how the prominent bass line interacts with the lush synth pads. In the Diana Ross original, producer Hal Davis mixed the live electric bass to hit every note with bluesy precision, making it the rhythmic foundation [CIT 10]. Doh recreates this effect not with a live player but with careful programming and EQ, meticulously carving out space in the mix so the synthesized bass provides the same foundational punch against a wall of electronic textures, a direct nod to the original's spatial and dynamic interplay.

source.title: Anatomy of THE Groove: “Love Hangover” by Diana Ross

nuggets.imageCaption: The 1976 Diana Ross album that established the song's foundational groove.

nuggets.headline: If the sonic texture of 'Love Hangover' feels authentic, it's because it shares its DNA with an unsung Motown session guitarist.
nuggets.text: The lush, disco-infused sound that Doh references is heavily indebted to Arthur G. Wright, a key session guitarist and engineer on the original Diana Ross track. Wright's guitar work was overdubbed in a late-night session, and his engineering techniques helped shape the iconic sound [CIT 22]. His fingerprint makes him a direct 'micro-genre ancestor' to Doh's track, a crucial behind-the-scenes influence only a deep dive into Motown's recording history would reveal.

source.title: Love Hangover by Diana Ross - Songfacts

nuggets.imageCaption: Session musician Arthur G. Wright, a key architect of the original's sound.

artistSummary: Aaron Doh's sonic identity is defined by a meticulous fusion of synth-pop architecture and modern digital production. Influenced by the layered, atmospheric approach of bands like CHVRCHES, his work on albums like "Love Lies" showcases a mastery of crafting lush, immersive soundscapes that are both polished and emotionally resonant [CIT 1]. Doh's methodology emphasizes the interplay between crisp digital precision and the warmth of vintage electronic textures.
